text:
This study addresses the critical issue of deep mining in China that is severely threatened by high-temperature hazards. The paper is divided into two main sections: airflow thermodynamic analysis and geothermal prediction. 

First, it begins with the observation and analysis of thermodynamic parameters in the mine, explaining the physical significance of temperature, pressure, wind speed, density, moisture content, relative humidity, enthalpy, and environmental thermal resistance. It highlights the interrelationships among these parameters and derived the thermodynamic variation of airflow from the surface to the mining working face, as well as the distribution of thermal and moisture sources based on observational data. 

Through an investigation of underground heat sources, a primary and secondary heat source map was established, identifying rock mass heat dissipation, thermal water discharge, and heat release from electromechanical equipment as the main heat sources that should be prioritized for management. The research also looked into methods for measuring temperature in boreholes and thermal adjustment loops in roadways, determining the geothermal gradient and the depth and temperature of the isothermal zone through temperature data. 

The paper introduces the concept of economic airflow for ventilation cooling, deriving its calculation model, and employs a simulated roadway method to establish the economically viable mining depth for the mine. It analyzes geothermal prediction methods and application principles, stating the basic data required to establish mathematical models. Using the 19 coal seam of the Panxi Coal Mine as a case study, it outlines the specific procedures for geothermal simulation and evaluates the geothermal conditions through the isothermal contour map of the 19 coal seam floor. 

A thermal analysis and geothermal assessment system for high-temperature mines has been developed, analyzing key technologies under development, as well as the structural composition, functional characteristics, and usage methods of the system. The study proposes measures and recommendations for controlling mine thermal hazards to guide the subsequent cooling efforts in the mine.

The airflow underground is derived from the surface, and thus the seasonal variations in surface atmospheric temperature and humidity inevitably influence the conditions below ground. The primary factors of surface atmospheric influence on the underground climate include surface atmospheric temperature, relative humidity, and atmospheric pressure. The patterns of change in surface atmospheric conditions impacting underground climate can be summarized as follows: ① Surface atmospheric temperature significantly affects the variation in underground air temperature; as surface temperatures change seasonally, the temperature at the mining face below also fluctuates accordingly, with a pronounced seasonal impact from surface temperatures. The effect of surface temperature on the air temperature at the underground mining face is particularly severe from April to October, especially notable from June to August; during the months of January to March and November to December, surface temperature has almost no influence on underground mining face air temperature; ② The relative humidity of the surface atmosphere has a distinct impact on the relative humidity of underground air; this influence is more pronounced from July to December, while from January to June, the relative humidity of the surface atmosphere has minimal effect on underground air humidity; ③ The effect of surface atmospheric pressure on underground air pressure is relatively minor, as underground air pressure varies in tandem with the depth of the mine.

As mining depth continues to increase, the mining environment has deteriorated, and the issues of high-temperature and high-humidity geothermal conditions in deep areas have gradually become prominent. This has led to worsening operational conditions, restricting deeper mining development and posing a serious threat to the safety of deep mines and the health of underground personnel, thereby increasing safety risks in mining operations. Therefore, effective control and management of geothermal hazards in deep mines is imperative, with the selection and evaluation of high-temperature hazard areas being particularly important. This paper uses the Chenzhou Mining Woxi mining area as the engineering background to analyze the causes of deep geothermal hazards and assess as well as manage high-temperature hazard areas. The main research content and findings are as follows:

1. By studying the heat sources causing high temperatures underground, this analysis identifies several major heat sources in deep mines under the mining environment, including heat transfer from surrounding rock, air compression heat, release of heat from hot water, heat from mechanical equipment, heat from transported materials, and heat from blasting. Additionally, the impact and hazards of deep mine geothermal hazards on underground workers are analyzed, providing a basis for evaluating high-temperature hazard areas in deep mines.

2. A shallow hole temperature measurement method was employed to investigate the original temperature of rocks in the Woxi mining area. Through linear regression analysis of the collected data, the geothermal warming rate for the Woxi mining area was determined. By comparing this with the geothermal warming rates of other domestic mines, it was found that the geothermal gradient in the Woxi mining area is relatively high, with severe deep geothermal hazards. Ten representative measurement points were selected to establish a geothermal monitoring system, providing data for the development of a fuzzy mathematical evaluation model for high-temperature hazard areas in deep mines.

3. Based on systematic principles for the established indicator evaluation system—representativeness, clarity and scientific validity, operability and quantifiability, and comprehensiveness—considering the complexity of deep heat sources, the evaluation system for high-temperature hazard areas in deep mines was categorized into target layer, indicator layer, and criterion layer. Nine major influencing factors, including mine temperature, were selected as evaluation indicators based on the subjective experiences of underground workers.

4. A comprehensive mathematical model for evaluating high-temperature hazard areas in deep mines was established based on an improved analytic hierarchy process (AHP) and fuzzy mathematics. The judgment matrix of the AHP was refined to determine the weight of each evaluation indicator. Standardized schemes were incorporated into the fuzzy mathematical model to compute and compare the merits of various schemes, while comparison with standardized schemes validated the feasibility of all approaches. This method was applied to the evaluation of high-temperature hazard areas in the Chenzhou Mining Woxi mining area, leading to a comprehensive evaluation of three different mid-level segments, resulting in evaluation vectors of (0.862, 0.777, 0.633, 0.831). The results indicate that as the location of the mine mid-segment deepens, the geothermal hazards intensify, with only the 16th level slightly better than the standard value, while the 42nd level presents severe geothermal hazards, failing to meet underground environmental standards. This is consistent with the actual conditions at the engineering site, demonstrating that the method is scientifically sound and can be promoted for engineering applications.

5. A management scheme for the existing high-temperature hazard areas at the mid-section has been proposed, including modifications to the ventilation system and multiple heat dissipation and cooling measures, providing important references for future effective management of geothermal hazards in the Woxi mining area.

Analyzing the types and causes of high-temperature heat sources in underground environments is essential for effectively implementing cooling measures. A precise study of these heat sources also provides guidance for managing thermal hazards in deep mines. Relevant literature indicates that the occurrence of high-temperature thermal hazards in mines results from various heat sources dissipating heat into the mine workings. Empirical evidence shows that geothermal energy (including heat transfer from surrounding rock and the release of heat from hot water), adiabatic compression of air, heat release from transporting minerals and rocks, heat emissions from electromechanical equipment, and heat from underground explosions are all primary underground heat sources. The high temperatures caused by heat transfer from surrounding rock significantly impact deep mining operations. With increasing mining depths, the geothermal temperature rise notably affects the surrounding rock temperature. Additionally, besides directly generating substantial heat, the heat from surrounding rock and air compression can indirectly lead to the formation of geothermal water in deep mines, resulting in excessive humidity in the underground environment due to evaporation, while also releasing large amounts of heat. In modern mines, due to the continuous enhancement of mechanization, particularly the dramatic increase in installed capacity at mining work faces, heat emissions from electromechanical equipment have become a significant heat source that cannot be overlooked. In ventilation systems utilizing transport tunnels as intake airways, the heat release from ore in transit represents a considerable heat source. Rock blasting, which is common in mining excavation operations, aims to break the rock and create tunnels; however, it also generates substantial heat that rapidly disperses into the air. The instantaneous heat generated during blasting causes the temperature of the rock at the blast face to rise significantly, directly affecting the temperature of the surrounding rock, thereby acting as a minor heat source itself. Beyond these factors, surface temperature and variations in air pressure also influence the temperature of the underground environment.

Deep well thermal hazards pose significant risks to underground workers. Analyzing the human body's heat balance equation reveals that the amount of heat retained by the body is related to metabolic rate, mechanical work done by the body, radiant heat exchange with the environment, convective heat transfer with the environment, and evaporative heat loss. From this analysis, it is evident that the heat exchange process for individuals working underground is intricate and complex. Due to the high temperature and humidity underground, which are significantly higher than surface conditions—where temperatures in some areas of deep mines can reach 30°C and relative humidity often exceeds 80%, with levels at the working face even hitting 100%—and the limited wind speed and airflow, the subjective thermal comfort of workers is adversely affected by this high-temperature, high-humidity, and low-wind-speed environment. This detrimental thermal hazard environment significantly impacts the health and work efficiency of underground personnel, leading to decreased attention, diminished reaction capabilities, reduced labor productivity, and an increased risk of major safety incidents.

This article first analyzes the underground thermal sources of the mine and introduces relevant calculations for their heat dissipation. On this basis, it examines the impact of thermal hazards on the human body, introduces both passive and mechanical cooling methods, and proposes the integration of the monitoring and control system from the "six major systems" of safety and prevention into the cooling system. Using the Gaofeng Mine as a case study, it analyzes the primary heat sources, calculates heat generation and cooling requirements, formulates a mechanical cooling scheme, and conducts reliability analysis and evaluation of the cooling effectiveness. The main research content and findings include: (1) categorizing underground thermal sources into relative and absolute heat sources, along with their respective heat generation calculation methods. The study investigates the effects of thermal hazards on the physiological, psychological, and work efficiency aspects of underground workers, and presents both non-mechanical and mechanical cooling methods. (2) Analyzing the basic principles and components of the monitoring and control system, proposing the application of this system to the mine’s cooling system, and studying the operational principles and equipment connection diagrams. (3) Taking the Gaofeng Mine as an example, the main heat sources identified underground are: heat release from surrounding rock, heat release from groundwater inflow, and heat dissipation from equipment. Based on field measurements, the cooling requirement for the -200m level is calculated to be approximately 1162.15 kW. (4) Based on the actual conditions of the Gaofeng Mine, ventilation optimization measures are proposed to increase the underground airflow, and a localized cooling scheme is designed, analyzing the system's structural composition, functional characteristics, and operational principles. The installation of two ZJL-450 type local cooling units at the -200m level can largely meet the cooling needs of the working face. (5) Reliability theory is applied to analyze the reliability of the localized cooling system at the Gaofeng Mine, concluding that the system's reliability is relatively high. The use of uncertain measurement theory assesses the effectiveness of the cooling system, revealing that the thermal comfort level of individuals in the -200m level improved from "rather uncomfortable" to "comfortable" following cooling measures, indicating satisfactory cooling performance.

Based on the investigation and analysis of thermal hazards in domestic mines, these hazards can be categorized into four types: normal geothermal increase type, thermal water geothermal anomaly type, rock temperature geothermal anomaly type, and sulfide mineral oxidation thermal type. 

I. Normal Geothermal Increase Type  
This type of thermal hazard occurs in mines located in areas with normal geothermal conditions, meaning that the heat flow values or geothermal gradient values are close to the average values of the crust's heat flow or geothermal gradient. The typical average heat flow value ranges from 1.4 to 1.5 heat flow units, while the average geothermal gradient is 1.5 to 3°C/100m². Generally, mines of this type do not experience thermal hazards when the mining depth is below 500-600m; however, upon reaching depths below 600m, the average rock temperature can exceed 35°C, leading to inevitable thermal hazards.

II. Thermal Water Geothermal Anomaly Type  
Thermal hazards of this type are primarily caused by deeply circulating geothermal water. The high flow rates and temperatures of the deep circulating water lead to geothermal gradient values that exceed the normal geothermal gradient.

III. Rock Temperature Geothermal Anomaly Type  
This type of thermal hazard mine is characterized by shallow geothermal anomalies in areas with geothermal anomalies, as well as localized geothermal anomalies within regions characterized by normal geothermal conditions. Currently, most of the mines exhibiting this type of thermal hazard in China are located within localized geothermal anomaly regions. The formation of these localized geothermal anomalies can result from various factors, including geological lithology at the Earth's surface, geological structures, paleoclimate conditions, and the presence of localized heat sources.

IV. Sulfide Mineral Oxidation Thermal Type  
Thermal hazards in this category arise from the oxidation reactions of sulfide minerals upon exposure to air during mining operations, which release heat, causing an increase in surrounding air temperature. The main hazards associated with this thermal hazard type include a deterioration of the working environment and labor conditions, the potential for high-temperature toxic heat waves, and the risk of sulfide dust explosions. Additionally, the considerable thermal intensity can affect the surrounding rock and rock anchoring systems. Workers may develop an energy-saving mentality, aiming to achieve maximum work efficiency with minimal energy consumption, leading them to neglect safety operating procedures and regulations, thereby increasing the risk of unsafe incidents.

Based on the investigation and analysis of geothermal hazards in domestic mines, geothermal hazards can be categorized into four types: Normal geothermal warming type, Hydrothermal geothermal anomaly type, Rock temperature geothermal anomaly type, and Sulfide mineral oxidation heat type.

1. Normal geothermal warming type  
This type of geothermal hazard occurs in mines situated in areas with normal geothermal conditions, meaning that the heat flow or geothermal gradient values are close to the average crustal heat flow or geothermal gradient values. The average heat flow value is typically between 1.4 to 1.5 heat flow units, and the average geothermal gradient is between 1.5 to 3°C/100m². Generally, mines of this type do not experience geothermal hazards when the mining depth is less than 500 to 600 meters; however, once the depth exceeds 600 meters, the average rock temperature reaches above 35°C, leading to the inevitable occurrence of geothermal hazards.

2. Hydrothermal geothermal anomaly type  
This type of geothermal hazard in mines is primarily caused by deep circulating groundwater. The substantial flow and high temperature of the circulating thermal water cause the geothermal gradient to exceed the normal geothermal gradient.

3. Rock temperature geothermal anomaly type  
Mines of this type are characterized by the presence of geothermal anomalies not only in the shallow parts of areas with regional geothermal anomalies but also in localized geothermal anomaly zones within normally geothermal areas. Most mines currently experiencing this type of geothermal hazard in China are found in localized geothermal anomaly zones. The formation of localized geothermal anomaly zones can be attributed to several factors, including surface geological rock types, geological structures, paleoclimatic conditions, and the presence of localized heat sources.

4. Sulfide mineral oxidation heat type  
This type of geothermal hazard occurs due to the oxidative reaction of sulfide minerals when exposed to air during mining, resulting in the release of heat that elevates the surrounding air temperature. The primary hazards associated with this type of geothermal impact include deterioration of the working environment and labor conditions for miners, the potential for high-temperature toxic heat waves and sulfur dust explosions, as well as thermal effects on surrounding rock and rock reinforcement systems.

The heating patterns of geothermal sources in the Gaofeng tin mine can be categorized into three forms: transient heat sources (such as heat from underground explosions), periodic heat sources (such as variations in surface atmospheric conditions), and persistent heat sources (such as heat transfer from surrounding rock, heat release from electromechanical equipment, and heat generated by air compression). Each of these heat sources has distinct impacts on the environment: ① Transient heat sources have a short heat release duration and have minimal effect on the underground climate; ② Periodically varying heat sources exhibit cyclical heat release changes, making them difficult to control, but sometimes play a regulatory role in the mine environment; persistent heat sources have a relatively constant effect on the underground environment, although the amplitude of heat release is decreasing, which makes them easier to manage from a control perspective. The "Mine Safety Regulations" stipulate that the air temperature at underground working sites must not exceed 26°C. Should it exceed this threshold, cooling measures or other protective actions should be implemented. During the investigation, the number of measurement points exceeding 26°C was recorded, focusing on high-temperature middle sections below 200 meters, where operational activities and personnel density are greater. The results of the investigation are presented in Table 4-2. The table indicates that, with increasing depth, the average temperature in each middle section gradually rises, with a more significant increase. Measurement points below the -60m middle section all exceed the prescribed environmental temperature limits, indicating severe thermal hazards.

Some metal mines in our country have begun deep well mining, and the phenomenon of "three highs" is gradually becoming apparent. Among these, the high-temperature environment in deep well mines has received increasing attention regarding its impact on production. This paper focuses on a large domestic copper mine, exploring the complete process of thermal environment management in metal mines. It addresses issues such as high surrounding rock temperatures in the underground workings, prolonged heating duration and high intensity of airflow passing through the tunnels, and severe thermal hazards at specific work points. A combination of field testing, theoretical analysis, and numerical simulation using 3DVS and Fluent software has been employed for a series of investigations. The main research contents of the paper are as follows: (1) Based on the practical experience of thermal hazard management at a specific copper mine, a survey method for the thermal environment of deep well high-temperature metal mines is developed. Field thermal environment testing is conducted to obtain underground thermal environment parameters. A qualitative analysis of the spatial variation patterns of the underground thermal environment in both vertical and horizontal dimensions is performed, and a quantitative analysis of the correlations between the main thermal environment parameters is carried out to establish a wind temperature prediction model based on thermal environment parameters; (2) By statistically analyzing the data from field tests, a scientific evaluation of the underground thermal environment is undertaken, determining the primary and secondary relationships of underground heat sources and identifying the locations with the most severe thermal hazards. A visualization model is constructed using Dimine software; (3) For locations experiencing severe underground thermal hazards, specific cooling solutions are proposed, and the cooling effects are further simulated using Fluent numerical simulation software to determine the optimal cooling airflow speed and temperature; (4) Concrete cooling methods are studied for the various degrees of thermal hazard issues encountered in different periods underground. The theoretical validation of whether ventilation cooling is fundamentally ineffective and the necessity of localized refrigeration cooling is fully discussed. A comprehensive analysis of the entire process of establishing a thermal environment management system that gradually integrates various cooling methods through years of ventilation and cooling practice is presented. The research results indicate that the heat dissipation from the ore and surrounding rock, alongside heat generation from mechanical equipment, accounts for 71.62% of the total heat dissipation underground, which is the primary cause of the high-temperature thermal environment. The most severely affected areas are the mining site and the excavation face. The spatial variation of the underground thermal environment exhibits certain patterns; however, significant fluctuations can occur on a local scale due to production activities. Various thermal environment parameters exhibit different degrees of correlation, and based on the statistical data obtained from underground measurements, a wind temperature prediction model can be constructed using Matlab software. The critical airflow speed and optimal temperature for cooling in the extracting and excavation work faces are measured at 0.75 m/s and 0.3 m/s, and 22°C and 23°C, respectively. On the basis of fully demonstrating that ventilation cooling cannot fundamentally resolve underground thermal hazard issues, the research suggests investigating localized or holistic artificial refrigeration cooling technologies, gradually forming a thermal environment management system that combines various cooling technologies with artificial refrigeration as an auxiliary measure.

Underground thermal hazards are an unavoidable natural disaster during deep well mining, and the research level of technologies for managing underground thermal hazards determines the mining depth of the mine. Advanced cooling technologies facilitate safe and efficient extraction from deep wells and contribute to achieving socio-economic benefits. The mine ventilation system consists of a ventilation network, ventilation power (fans and natural air pressure), ventilation equipment, and structures, which are responsible for supplying fresh air underground and eliminating harmful gases. With the improvement of mining and selection technologies and the depletion of shallow resources, ore extraction is gradually entering the era of deep well mining, where many old mines are expanding deeper. This makes the ventilation systems increasingly complex, as the new and old ventilation systems interact, necessitating a new dynamic equilibrium, resulting in growing challenges; the most direct manifestation is the deterioration of the underground environment and rising temperatures. Ventilation plays a crucial role in cooling the mine; if ventilation is poor and airflow is insufficient, heat cannot be expelled, leading to heat accumulation, which accelerates the oxidation and heat dissipation of the ore, forming a vicious cycle. Additionally, during the mine development phase, due to the absence of a complete ventilation system, thermal hazards are most severe, especially at the excavation face, where heat exchange occurs with the airflow, resulting in a continuous rise in air temperature. When a certain point is reached, the heat exchange between the two tends to stagnate; if new low-temperature airflow does not enter or the incoming airflow is insufficient, heat cannot be rapidly expelled. Consequently, the main sources of heat underground are issues within the ventilation network, and research on cooling is intrinsically linked to mine ventilation, making ventilation and cooling the primary focus in the management of thermal hazards.

There are many heat sources causing the increase in mine air temperature, primarily categorized as relative heat sources and absolute heat sources. The heat dissipation of relative heat sources depends on the temperature difference with their surroundings, such as heat dissipation from high-temperature strata and hot water; whereas the heat dissipation of absolute heat sources is less affected by the air temperature, such as heat from electromechanical equipment, chemical reactions, and air compression. The heat dissipation from high-temperature strata is a significant reason for the increase in mine air temperature, mainly occurring through thermal exchange between the walls of the shaft and the fallen, transported ore with the air; additionally, the influx of high-temperature water in the mine can also affect the microclimate of the entire mine, leading to a slight increase in mine air temperature. Overall, the main factors contributing to the high-temperature thermal hazards in mines include geothermal heat, the heat released during the operation of mining electromechanical equipment, heat released from minerals and gangue during transportation, and heat release due to self-compression of airflow during descent. For specific mines, the emergence of high-temperature water and intense oxidation of minerals can also lead to high-temperature thermal hazards. Other factors contributing to increased mine temperatures include: firstly, the significant mining depth leading to elevated rock temperatures. In the central and northern regions of China, most high-temperature mines are caused by such factors. Secondly, the influx of underground hot water. Because underground hot water is easy to flow and has a high heat capacity, it serves as an excellent medium for heat transfer, primarily transmitting heat to the airflow through two pathways: ① Hot water in the rock strata heats the surrounding rock through convection, which then transfers heat to the airflow; ② Hot water flows into the mine passages, directly heating the airflow; ③ Low airflow at the mining face. Poor ventilation and low airflow are common factors currently causing higher air temperatures at mining faces in China. According to investigations, over 80% of the airflow supplied to longwall working faces in Chinese coal mines is between 200-800 m³/min, whereas, according to thermal requirements, the airflow supply for high-temperature extraction working faces should be at least over 800 m³/min.

The phenomenon of thermal hazards becomes severe after deep mining in geothermal anomalous mines. Through the analysis of the thermal field, temperature gradient variations, and the thermal physical properties of rocks at the Sanhejian mine, it reveals that the deep temperature anomalies increase with depth, and the heat flow value is notably high, indicative of a typical geothermal anomalous mine. The analysis summarizes the characteristics of high-temperature thermal hazards in the Sanhejian model, specifically a geothermal anomaly caused by a shortage of cold sources. 

For managing the thermal hazards in the Sanhejian model, considering the current surface heating situation of the mine, a cooling technology known as HEMS is proposed, which utilizes underground thermal water for surface heating, storing the resulting cold energy in the Quaternary energy storage layer for underground cooling. This approach addresses thermal hazards while providing surface heating. The Sanhejian mine has successfully implemented the first phase of underground thermal hazard management, and the analysis of the cooling effects on the 72201 working face shows that this system greatly improves the long-standing high temperature and humidity working environment, making it worthy of promotion in deep mining and related fields.

This study summarizes the existing research findings related to geological hazards from three aspects: the disaster forms of mining geothermal, heat damage control technologies, and methods for utilizing thermal energy. The results indicate that the disaster forms caused by mining geothermal include three categories: exacerbation of coal and rock mass property deterioration, triggering of support structure failure, and creation of high-temperature and high-humidity environments. Specifically, this includes seven aspects: aggravation of surrounding rock deformation damage, triggering gas desorption, reducing anchor bolt anchoring strength, accelerating corrosion of support materials, harming workers' physical and mental health, reducing work efficiency, and increasing mechanical equipment failure rates. Heat damage control technologies are divided into two types: non-artificial cooling technologies and artificial cooling technologies. Among non-artificial cooling technologies, there are three categories: heat source control technologies, heat and humidity environment regulation technologies, and individual protective technologies. According to the differences in refrigerants, artificial refrigeration systems can be classified into three main categories: air-cooled, ice-cooled, and water-cooled systems, which include compressed air refrigeration, ice refrigeration, surface centralized refrigeration, surface heat dissipation wells for centralized cooling, returned air heat dissipation wells for centralized cooling, surface combined heat and power refrigeration, and the resource utilization of heat damage. Currently, the main method of utilizing mining geothermal is extracting residual heat from mine water and return air for frost prevention at the mine entrance, bathing, and building heating. In recent years, achieving mine cooling while directly extracting thermal energy from surrounding rock is a research hotspot and a critical aspect of direct geothermal utilization. Arranging buried pipe heat exchangers in backfilled materials or surrounding rocks within the tunnels to extract heat energy and achieve the simultaneous utilization of various clean energy sources in the mining area is one of the future directions for the development of mining geothermal utilization.

As mining operations shift deeper, high geothermal heat hazards have become a significant challenge for the safe extraction of resources. Mine ventilation and air conditioning are gradually becoming the primary forms of energy consumption in the exploitation of deep mineral resources, impacting the operational costs of mines and the sustainability of resource development. This paper outlines the evolution of heat transfer theory in mine ventilation and the current state of cooling technology research in mines, analyzing the characteristics of commonly used cooling technologies. Based on this analysis, it also envisions key development directions for regulating the thermal environment of deep mines in the future. The results indicate that mechanical refrigeration ventilation is currently the main method for managing thermal hazards in mines, yet it has drawbacks such as high costs, difficult maintenance, low cooling efficiency, and suboptimal cooling effects. To enhance the effectiveness of thermal hazard management in deep mines, a new approach of "source reduction and flow optimization" should be adopted, implementing proactive cooling measures during the mining process and utilizing multiple methods for combined cooling. Additionally, integrating intelligent ventilation systems to precisely control temperature and reduce refrigeration energy consumption, employing circulating ventilation cooling technology to improve cooling efficiency, and using high-performance cooling protective gear for specific high-temperature scenarios are recommended. Transforming traditional thermal hazard management techniques into geothermal resource utilization concepts to achieve a synergy between thermal environment regulation and geothermal resource extraction is key to efficiently addressing deep mine cooling and upgrading the energy structure of mining systems.

The high-temperature environment in underground settings not only reduces the operational performance of equipment and shortens its lifespan but also poses significant physiological and psychological harm to underground workers. This environment can easily affect work efficiency and trigger safety incidents, as illustrated in Figure 1. 

Physiologically, high temperatures can lead to an increased respiratory rate and heart rate, elevated blood pressure, heightened fatigue, and a 3.61-fold increase in disturbances to the central nervous system. Psychologically, miners who work in these harsh high-temperature conditions may face a series of issues. The incidence of illness among personnel working in high-temperature environments can reach alarming levels compared to normal conditions, leading to negative psychological responses such as reactance, apathy, and risk-taking behaviors, accompanied by symptoms of emotional distress and irritability. These emotions can easily lead to unsafe behaviors.

In terms of work efficiency, high temperatures can exacerbate fatigue, diminish memory retention, reduce concentration, and delay reaction times. Furthermore, the high-temperature environment can severely impact the safe and efficient operation of machinery, decreasing overall operational efficiency and shortening service life. 

Regarding production safety, workers in high-temperature, high-humidity mines experience neural suppression, which gradually diminishes their awareness of the surrounding environment. Additionally, elevated ground temperatures can increase the release of toxic and harmful gases from rock strata. As mental acuity, judgment, and reaction capabilities decline, the incidence of safety accidents rises due to thermal hazards, rock collapses, spontaneous fires, and other secondary disaster threats—notably, the risks of combustible gas explosions, rockfalls, and spontaneous combustion-related fires.

The depth and temperature of the Daqiang Mine constant temperature zone are 20 m and 9.5°C, respectively, with an average geothermal gradient of the mine being 3.52~3.27°C/100 m, and the vast majority of the geothermal gradient exceeding 3°C/100 m. The horizontal vertical depth of the main shaft reaches 1,000 m, and based on the geothermal gradient, the calculated temperature is 43°C, while the measured temperature reaches as high as 58°C. The main reasons for the geothermal anomaly at the Daqiang Mine are: 1) the significant depth of the mine, which results in inherently high coal-rock temperatures; 2) the complex geological structure of the coalfield, characterized by numerous faults, which facilitate the communication of heat flow from the upper mantle to shallower depths, leading to increased rock temperatures; 3) heat sources generated during mining activities, mainly including heat release from surrounding rock, oxidation heat from the mined-out areas, and mechanical heat dissipation. 

Measures for managing thermal hazards in mines can be categorized into two main types: non-artificial cooling of underground airflow and mechanical air conditioning cooling. Specifically, these measures include the following: 1) Increasing airflow. In cases where thermal hazards are not severe, increasing ventilation can help lower underground temperatures. However, airflow augmentation is not limitless; it is constrained by prescribed air speeds and cooling costs, and its cooling effect diminishes and eventually disappears beyond a certain airflow increase. 2) Reforming ventilation methods. Converting the updraft to a downdraft can be beneficial for reducing air temperature. 3) Avoiding localized heat sources. Various local heat sources, such as heat dissipation from electrical equipment, thermal water dissipation, mineral oxidation heat release, and air leakage in mined-out areas, contribute to heating the airflow. Therefore, it is essential to analyze the heat sources in the mine and implement targeted measures to reduce heat emission, ensuring that fresh airflow avoids these localized heat sources to minimize temperature and humidity increases. 4) Pre-cooling incoming airflow. Utilizing non-mechanical cooling methods to lower the temperature of airflow entering the working face. 5) Isolating high-temperature surrounding rock. When the temperature of the surrounding rock is very high, certain insulation materials should be sprayed onto the rock walls to prevent heat from the surrounding rock from dissipating into the air of the tunnel; however, current applications are limited. 6) Individual protection. In certain locations with adverse climatic conditions within the mine, workers may wear cooling suits for personal protection. 7) Mechanical cooling.

Deep mining is the future direction of mining technology, and mining thermal hazards are one of the main factors restricting safe production in deep mines. Due to the working face of deep mines being located within the geothermal gradient zone, compounded by underground industrial heat sources, these heat sources exhibit characteristics such as high density and dispersion, prolonged duration, and high instantaneous heat release power. Conventional cooling technologies, including ventilation optimization, roadway and equipment layout optimization, and heat source isolation, cannot meet the cooling demands of deep mines, highlighting the urgent need for the development and application of efficient, eco-friendly, high-capacity underground artificial refrigeration technologies. 

Starting from the principle of heat pumps, this study discusses the temporal and spatial distribution characteristics of heat sources and sinks in the surface and underground of the mining area, summarizing the challenges of applying artificial refrigeration technologies in mitigating thermal hazards in deep mines. The Guizhou Jinfeng Gold Mine currently reaches a mining depth of 630 meters. Using this mine as a case study, the sources of underground thermal hazards were identified and the cooling load calculated. Based on the production characteristics of the mine, cooling targets were selected. The feasibility of utilizing nearby water bodies as thermal sinks for underground cooling through water-source heat pump technology was explored. 

Through field surveys, measurements, theoretical calculations, and numerical simulations, the heat storage capacity and thermal sink stability of four water bodies—Greenshade Pond, Luofan River, Beipan River, and the production water diversion channel—near the mining area were analyzed. The initial investment and operating costs based on different water-source heat pump schemes were calculated, followed by a technical-economic comparison. After comprehensive analysis, the recommendation was made to utilize the mine's water diversion channel as a water-source heat pump cooling source for one-time cooling of the mine's fresh air intake. This solution is characterized by effective cooling performance, low investment, high thermal sink stability capacity, and minimal ecological impact.

As the shallow resources in our country are nearly exhausted, deep mining will become the norm. Mine thermal hazards represent a significant bottleneck in deep mining operations, facing a series of challenges during their management, including high costs and unsatisfactory mitigation effects. Although geothermal heat in mines can cause thermal hazards, it is also a valuable source of thermal energy. The synergistic extraction of mineral geothermal energy is an important approach to "turning harm into benefit and waste into treasure," as well as a significant initiative in exploring green energy extraction. 

1) Mine thermal hazards result from a combination of factors including heat release from surrounding rock, heat released by thermal groundwater, and heat from airflow compression. In deep mines, the heat release from these sources is greater and more difficult to control, thereby posing a severe challenge of high-temperature thermal hazards for deep, high-temperature mines. Long-term work in high-temperature environments negatively impacts physiological and psychological health, work efficiency, and production safety. Addressing mine thermal hazards is not only a technical requirement for deep mining but also reflects a commitment to the welfare of the mining labor force. 

2) During the management of thermal hazards in deep mines, a comprehensive approach centered on "source reduction and flow control" should be adopted, where local cooling methods are implemented alongside comprehensive control of heat sources. Mines can establish a ventilation system that integrates display systems, underground detection and monitoring systems, information analysis and processing systems, and intelligent control systems to smartly regulate mine ventilation, quickly adjusting localized airflow to improve cooling efficiency and alleviate thermal hazards. Utilizing human recognition and sensing technology for precise temperature control of specific target areas, along with the implementation of movable air curtains for isolating cooling zones, can significantly enhance local cooling effectiveness and reduce cooling loss. 

3) This paper proposes the concept of synergistic extraction of mineral geothermal energy, where geothermal extraction in mines can lead to substantial economic benefits while also helping to mitigate mine thermal hazards. Synergistic extraction of mineral geothermal energy can involve heat storage and extraction from mining layers, rock layer heat extraction, and waste heat recovery. The extracted geothermal energy can be efficiently utilized for various applications such as bathing, building heating, greenhouse cultivation, and aquaculture. Numerical simulations indicate that heat extraction from surrounding rock in the tunnels rapidly lowers airflow temperature within the tunnels, effectively addressing mine thermal hazards.
